title: Trump's War on Iran / Davos / Canada-China Relations w/ Prof. Jiang Xueqin of Predictive History
full_text: Good day. This is Demetri Lceris coming to you for Reason to Resist from Montreal, Canada on January 22nd, 2026. Today I'm very pleased to be joined by Professor Ciang. Professor Ciang is a Chinese Canadian educator, writer, historian, and geopolitical theorist based in Beijing, China. He's also the founder and host of a widely followed and important YouTube channel called Predictive History. Thanks very much for joining us for the first time, Professor Jen. >> Thanks so much, Demet. I'm a huge fan of your work. >> Uh, thank you so much. Um, first, uh, you know, I wanted to discuss with you initially when I reached out to you uh, Mark Carney's, uh, just completed trip to Beijing and also his widely discussed uh, speech at Davos. But, uh, in light of events over the last couple of days, I've added to the agenda uh, the looming attack or what appears to be a looming attack on Iran. uh and uh I'm going to start with that subject. Before I do, I just like to remind our audience to like and share this video if they find it informative and uh to uh please subscribe to our YouTube channel if uh you haven't already done so. So with that, uh professor, I want to show you an excerpt from an interview this week of retired US General Jack Keane. In this clip from uh a Fox News interview, Keen is asked whether he believes uh that an attack on Iran by the Trump regime is imminent. And this is what he had to say. >> Sure. >> Oh. Oh, okay. >> And then there's no doubt about it. I mean, we're bringing these resources to bear, and it's likely because we had to expand the target set. And why are we expanding it? Because we have to consider what is Iran going to do in retaliation? Our bases are vulnerable. What are they going to do about Israel? They're going to attack Israel likely. >> And they're vulnerable. And what are we going to do about their ballistic missiles? Are we going to take them down before they fire them at our bases and also at Israel? Likely. Are we going to kill leaders? Certainly. Our IRGC members, the people that are oppressing the people, absolutely. So, I think the target set is expanded. resources are being brought in to deal with an expanded target set, which is what we do. >> That's right, folks. That's what we do. Uh now last week, Professor Jiang, uh you gave an interview, I believe it was on, uh Danny Hiong uh in which you talked about the possibility of an attack on Iran. And um you uh expressed the view as I understand it that uh the US government would have considerable difficulty toppling the current government of Iran and um that would more likely to turn instead to uh easy winds in places like Greenland, Canada, and Cuba. Um, I'm curious to know whether in light of what has transpired over the last two to three days and in light also of uh retired General Keane's commentary, has your assessment changed? Do you think that an attack on Iran is not likely at this stage or do you think one is virtually inevitable? >> Yeah. So, let me clarify my comments. So, so my feeling is that they will attack Iran. Uh, they will use air strikes. It will be very impressive. They will hit a lot of military targets. be very sustained, but air strikes by themselves are not effective. And eventually Trump's going to halt air strikes and move his attention elsewhere, but then he'll resume air strikes at a later point. So I think that um this US Iran conflict will be uh in the background throughout 2026. Um, unless Trump is willing to commit ground troops, he cannot actually achieve any of his war aims in uh Iran. The regime is far too resilient, far too resourceful, uh far too cohesive. So that's what I meant. I I I do think that Trump will strike uh Iran because that's always been part of the plan. Remember back in his first term, there's a clear trend towards confrontation with Iran. So Trump immediately withdrew from the nuclear deal. Uh then he um transferred the US embassy from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem which is extremely provocative. Uh he initiated something called the Abraham Accords uh which sought to broad peace between Israel and Saudi Arabia. And the most provocative thing he did of course was in June 2020 he authorized the assassination of General Salammani who was the Iranian de facto envoy in the Middle East. And that essentially is a declaration of war. So if he had sent him office then we would have seen uh mil military strikes against Iran. He lost in 2020 but now he's back in the second term. Uh he is going to continue his the policies uh from his first term which is to seek regime change in Iran. Mhm. Uh I would add to that list, by the way, and I'm sure you're aware of this, uh the uh recognition by the Trump regime in the first administration of uh Israel's theft of the Syrian Golan Heights. Uh we could probably go on and on, but uh I think people get the picture. Now, Iran has long warned that if pushed, it's willing uh and able to close the Strait of Hormuz. If it did that for a significant period of time, let's say 6 months or even more, what would you expect the impact on the global economy to be? So if Iran were to close all the surf moves, it would really be the nuclear option. It'd be worse than a nuclear bomb because it would paralyze the global economy. So most of Southeast Asia, specifically China, uh Japan and South Korea depend on most of its oil and energy from the Middle East. Um, and so if that were to happen, then these economies were to shutter and they would have to respond militarily, especially China and Japan. Um, and so that that really is the trump card that Iran can play. So I imagine that we still have pretty far to go on the escalation ladder before Iran um plays that trump card. So, I imagine what's going to happen um and it's just my best guess as to what's going to happen is that Trump's going to authorize um air strikes and then uh is uh Iran is going to retaliate against Israel because Israel doesn't participate in the air strikes. It's also going to Iran's is also going to retaliate against uh local um Middle East allies including Jordan. Um and uh Iran will strike at uh US air uh US bases in the region for its proxies. um Hezbollah and the Hufi specifically. So um I believe there's an escalation ladder and we have to climb pretty far up the escalation ladder before Iran closes a few moves because if they would do that this would basically be World War II. Uh it would draw in a lot of nations and um the consequences could is it's not really contained anymore. Uh it would be very unpredictable and unprecedented if if Iran were a close officer of Humus. Now, uh, a couple of days ago, uh, a publication called Defense Security Asia, uh, published an article stating that there were unconfirmed reports, that's the way they described them, of a rapid Chinese military airlift into Iran involving up to 16 cargo aircraft. That would be quite a haul potentially, of military equipment. What do you make of these reports, Professor Jiang? >> Right. So from the Chinese perspective, um what Trump is really doing is initiating a long-term strategy of strangling China economically. If you read the national security strategy which came out last month, it's very explicit that um um the United States cannot challenge uh China in the southeast uh Asia militarily. So what what it's going to do is that it's going to use its proxies like Japan and it's going to strangle uh China economically. Now the problem the problem with China is that um if it's its major strength is its exports. So if the United States were to close off the first island chain, right, which is Japan, Taiwan, the Philippines, um, and if there were a war in Iran, then China would be strangled economically because it could not access uh, oil. So I know that Russia is providing 20% of um, China's energy needs, but 20% is not a lot. And the reality is that Russia is having problems meeting that demand right now. So, it's going to take, you know, 5 to 10 years before the Trans Siberian pipeline is um online and Russ and Russia can provide more energy to China. And that's why the United States is going to strike now from a Chinese perspective. I'm saying this this from a Chinese military perspective that the real reason that Trump is going after Iran is to destroy Chinese economy. So, here China have no choice but to bolster Iran. And um so they're they're going to send weapon systems, they're going to send financing, they're going to do whatever they can uh to support Iran. Um but but but there are some red lines. So China will not send soldiers and China will not uh declare war on the United States. >> Do you think that China might send naval vessels into the region? >> Yeah. So the issue is that um China doesn't have a blue water navy to challenge um the American Navy, the blue water navy. But I think what's going to happen this year is that very quickly Russia is going to transform its shuttle fleet. It's it's about a thousand ships into a blue water navy. There's no way that Russia can defeat America in the oceans. Uh but Russia at least has to challenge America. It basically has to um basically um create a war of attrition in the seas. that would hamper uh America's aggression against Iran and China would provide financing for Russia's bluewater navy. So I think that's the what's most likely to happen. >> So let's turn now to uh a less uh violent topic, namely the subject of Mark Carney's much discussed speech at Davos. Uh the most widely discussed aspect of this speech, I think, is Carney's acknowledgement that the rules-based order is, as he put it, a partial fiction. Uh now, he didn't uh that implies that some part of it was true, and he didn't tell us which part, if any, of the rules-based order was true or based on reality. But in any case, um he went on to explain what he believed should replace the rules-based order. and he referred to this new foreign policy paradigm as values-based realism which he said actually came from Finland's president Alexander Stub and in the following excerpt from Carney's Davos speech Carney explains what he means by values-based realism what does it mean for middle powers to live the truth first it means naming reality stop invoking rules-based international order as though it still functions as advertised call Call it what it is, a system of intensifying great power rivalry where the most powerful pursue their interests using economic integration as coercion. It means acting consistently, applying the same standards to allies and rivals. When middle powers criticize economic intimidation from one direction but stay silent when it comes from another, we are keeping the sign in the window. It means building what we claim to believe in rather than waiting for the old order to be restored. It means creating institutions and agreements that function as described. And it means reducing the leverage that enables coercion. >> Now, Professor, we just heard Mark Carney say that values-based realism means applying the same standards to allies and rivals. Uh, and I must confess that I'm deeply skeptical that Canada will now apply the same standards to the West's allies and rivals. And I'm going to give but one example, and that is within 48 hours of Carney delivering this speech, Israel murdered 11 Palestinians in the Gaza Strip, including three more journalists and two more children. Uh, as you can see from this uh, report uh, by Thompson Reuters, I've seen no statement from any Canadian government official condemning or even expressing concern about these atrocities. Uh, they have been virtually silent the entire time that Israel has killed over 100 children in what is supposed to be a ceasefire. Uh moreover, after more than two years of genocide, Mark Carney's government has imposed no sanctions on Netanyahu or Galand, both of whom have been indicted by the ICC. It has imposed no sanctions on major Israeli corporations, not even those in the uh military-industrial sector. It's imposed no sanctions on Israel's military leaders, but it continues to maintain sweeping sanctions on Russia and Iran, uh both of which it regards as geopolitical rivals of the West. Uh so again to be perfectly blunt about a professor I think that Mark Carney's speech was essentially a marketing exercise. That's my suspicion and I hope I'm wrong. Uh he knows that the whole world now sees the reality of the rules-based international order. Uh that it is a fraud and that the concept has been discredited and therefore I suspect and I could be wrong again that western leaders want to rebrand uh their foreign policy as a values-based realism. But there doesn't appear to be any meaningful distinction between that and the now completely discredited rules-based international order. So that's basically my skeptical view. Uh but I'm very curious to know what is your take not just on this particular question more broadly on Mark Carney's speech at Davos. >> Yeah. So um overall I agree your sentiment. Um I I think you tend to be very right about these sort of things. It's better to be skeptical than it is to be um naive. Right. So um I I did read Mark Carney's speech and he makes in his speech two major historical illusions. The first is to facilities and facilities is writing about the pel pelpian war and the war was fought between Athens and Sparta. So um Athens um had uh defeated the Persians and because of that the um uh the uh Aianc Greeks were very thankful to the Athenians just as the Europeans were very thankful to the Americans for defeating fascism in Europe. And so um after the Persian wars they created something called the Delian League. It was meant to be a mutual defense pack. But over time Athens as a hedgeimon started to exploit its allies. it it became a mafia regime and it started to extort its allies. So, and then its allies had no choice but to seek relief from Sparta. So, I think that with this Fidili's illusion, Mark Carney is is sort of like hinting to his audience that we are at this moment when uh America's former allies have turned into their va vassels. And so, America is going to exploit us. America is going to steal our wealth. America is going to fight make us fight wars we cannot possibly win. um probably in Ukraine and so we need to band together and seek relief from another Sparta. Today Sparta would be China, right? So I think that's the first historical illusion he's making. The second historical illusion he's making of course is to Valhava's famous uh essay the p the power of the powerless. Um and and you know it's very ironic he does that because uh in his speech he says that okay uh let's be honest from day one we knew this rulesbased national order was a fraud it was hypocritical but we benefit from it okay because we were friends of the hegeimon the um great empire and so we benefited but now that the empire is turning against us Now that the empire wants to cannibalize us, we must now speak truth to power. Okay. So you can imagine the hypocrisy. Um so I don't think it's it's much of a strategy. Okay. Because I think like anyone with a brain, you know, which obviously precludes Canadians, but anyone with a brain can see through the hypocrisy, the travesty um and the self-indulgence in his speech. Um it's not really a grand strategy. It's more like it's more of a lamont, right? It's it's lamentation of, you know, like we used to be best friends America. We benefited a great deal. Now America has abandoned us. We're a jud lover and now we're forced to uh band together and seek a new ally in China. So I read the speech more like that. Um you know and he has like three grand suggestions for moving forward. The first of course is to speak truth to power to recognize the severity of the situation and be honest with ourselves. The second is to create as you say um uh a new system uh of rules-based international order of institutions that could counterbalance the bullying of America. And the third is to uh rely more heavily on Canadian capital and talent. The problem Canada you know this is that everyone's fleeing right capital fleeing talent is fleeing Canada. So Canada's being hollowed out. So um you know as a banker and you know Mark Carney used to work for Goldman Sachs uh he used to be governor of the bank of Canada and then he went to uh England became the first foreign governor of the bank of England. He is a banker who serves transnational capital and the reality is that Canada uh the nation I hate to say this because I grew up there right um but Canada it's a toxic asset right now. housing prices are are like, you know, skyhigh. Um the Canadian economy, 40% of the Canadian economy um is real estate speculation. Uh Canada is this glorified money laundering operation. Um it's lost all its legitimacy by by supporting this genocide in Gaza. Um there are hundreds of thousands of homeless in Canada. Yet Carney still continue to send tens of billions of dollars to Ukraine to fight a pointless war causing you know about a million Ukrainians to die for no particular reason. Um these um Canadian companies refuse to invest in the country and many of them are fleeing the country. So Canada is a toxic asset and what you do with toxic asset as a banker is you sell it to foreigners. And who are the foreigners? The Qataris and the Chinese. Okay. So that's the grand strategy that Carney has and everything else like this grand rhetoric about a rules based national order. It's just as you say marketing. That's all it is. Okay. Don't pay any attention to it. Watch what he does. So what he's going to do is this. He's going to call in an early election probably late spring. Okay. April. He's going to win by his landslide. He's have a super majority and then he'll start to asset strip Canada. Okay? That's the plan. Pretty simple. >> That's quite distressing for those of us who live here. But I I find that an entirely uh persuasive uh prediction about what is likely to happen in the months ahead. Uh now let's let's imagine uh Professor Jiang that you uh were uh asked to provide advice to the Canadian government about how to deal with the current predicament. You know, I know this is a huge and complex question, but I am curious to know uh how you would uh deal with it. And let me be clear. I've been complaining for years that our governments, successive liberal and conservative governments have done so much to increase our dependency upon the United States and to de-industrialize this company, to deprive this country, uh to deprive us of the capacity to produce domestically the things that are necessary for the well-being of the Canadian population. Um but this is the world we're now in. We have an extraordinary degree of dependency on the United States. We have a lunatic in the White House uh who o openly talks about turning Canada into the 51st state. There's no way even with the ruinous uh uh you know plan to increase Canada's military spending to 5% of GDP that we could compete with the United States in terms of military spending. We have the longest undefended border in the world with the United States. Um we're in a bit of a pickle. So in these cir circumstances, how would you recommend the Canadian government deal with the new reality? >> Okay. So realistically, there's nothing Canada can really do to stop America from colonizing Canada. There just really isn't. Um so and and America doesn't have to use it military because all America has to do is propose um that Canadians can have American passports and that whatever Canadian savings they have will be converted to American dollars onetoone. Okay. So so Canadians are getting a huge huge uh discount. The reality is that 25% of Canadians are foreign born and most of them do not have any loyalties to uh Canada. Um, Canada also has this bloed exploited bureaucracy, federal bureaucracy where 22 22% of Canadians work for the government in some capacity. Um, entrepreneur energy is dead. If you're if you're entrepreneur, you are burdened by too many taxes, by too much regulation, and so you flee um to to the American Americans. So there's if there were ever a referendum, you'd be surprised by the number of Canadians who vote to join the United the United States. Uh the big I I I think the big uh vulnerability for Canada is Alberta. The the Albertans are not very happy with the fact that they're basically subsidizing all of Canada and um the push for independence is now very very strong and in Alberta and Trump could make um can take advantage of that by supporting the this push. So there are a lot of vulnerabilities in in Canada. Um but I'll answer your question directly like what would I suggest? Okay. >> Okay. Well, first of all, the problem is that Canadians live in a bubble and Canadians have an unfortunate tendency to put their heads in the sand at the worst times. And so um the idea that government would actually bring an outside advisor who would tell them bad news, it's very inconceivable. But, you know, let's just say let's just for the uh let's for the sake of argument just say that they bring me in and ask me what I should do, what what what I would do. Okay? And here's my advice. And I know this is counterintuitive, but my advice is Canadians um should love Trump. They should make Trump into a god. They should have statues of Trump everywhere. Okay? They should put Trump on TV all the time in school. All Canadian children should love to uh worship Trump as a king. Forget King Charles. We don't want him anymore. We want we want King Trump to be the sovereign of Canada. And I guarantee you if can Canadians were to do that, all the problems would go away and in fact their lives would improve. But guess what? They're not going to do that. Okay. >> Well, no. That actually brings us to our next topic, which is Mark Carney's trip to Beijing. That doesn't seem to be the kind of thing that is going to uh uh win the goodwill and good graces of King Donald, but I want to get into the particulars of what happened there. Um and uh you know when Mark Carney uh completed his meetings with uh Chinese President Xi Jinping, he issued a statement his government did which touted what he described as a new strategic partnership with China and I'm just going to put up on the screen here uh what I think is the most uh informative part of the statement and I've highlighted in uh blue the only aspects of the agreements that were struck in China which translate into concrete uh improvements in the terms of trade between the two countries. Uh I wasn't able to identify others. There were numerous agreements apparently which stated you know the party's commitments to various objectives and the goodwill and their hopes and aspirations. But the actual meat on the bones, this is what I was able to identify and I'm quoting to help deliver the full potential of these partnerships and build up our domestic manufacturing sector. Canada will allow up to 49,000 Chinese electrical vehicles into the Canadian market with MFN tariff rate of 6.1%. This corresponds to volumes in the year prior to recent trade frictions on these imports representing less than 3% of the Canadian market for new vehicles in Canada. Uh the uh I believe the number for 2024 was somewhere in the range of 28 million vehicles sold in Canada. So this is a really small proportion. Um it is expected that within three years this agreement will drive considerable new Chinese joint venture investment in Canada with trusted partners, protect and create new automanufacturing jobs blah blah blah. And then he goes on and says um oh I should also just specify uh that uh in this paragraph it stated that it's also anticipated in 5 years more than 50% of these vehicles will be affordable EVs with an import price of less than 35%. Somewhere else I read that half of this very low quota uh is for vehicles less than $35,000 and from what I understood the rest have to be at a price point above 35,000. In any case, it goes on and says, "Agra, food and trade are foundations of a long-standing relationship can between Canada and China. And uh Canada expects that by March 1st, 2026, can China will lower tariffs on Canadian canola seed to a combined rate of approximately 15 uh%. China is a $4 billion canola seed market for Canadian producers. And this change represents a significant drop from the current combined tariff levels of approximately 85%. And Canada expects the Canadian canola meal, lobsters, crabs, and peas will not be subject to relevant anti-discrimination tariffs from March 1st, 2026 until at least the end of the year. So that's 10 months now. Uh all this strikes me as rather modest, Professor Chang, uh I don't see this as being any profound change in the trading relationship between Canada and China. Uh I I I guess I would concede that it is a significant improvement and I certainly would love to see Canada uh pursue closer relations in all aspects uh with uh the People's Republic of China. Uh but I don't see this as being a strategic partnership and I curious uh as to your thoughts as to how important this actually is and also secondarily how you anticipate that the Trump regime is going to respond to this. Right. So, um I would say this is a breakthrough and I and I would say this will lead to uh bigger strategic partnerships very quickly down the line. Okay. So, like there are different aspects to what's going on. The first aspect is that Trump has basically declared war on Latin and South America. Okay. Okay. So you have countries like Brazil, uh Colombia, Mexico that are not that are not obeying him, that are not being sufficiently deferential to him. Okay. Now, um Brazil exports a lot of agricultural products to uh China, especially soy beans. And Trump is angry about that as well because remember when um Trump in uh imposed tariffs on China, China responded by uh cutting uh agriculture imports from the United States which hurts farmers in America which supposedly hurts the mega base and then China started to um import a lot of agricultural products from Brazil. So what uh Trump wants to do is basically strangle uh the uh South America and prevent trade between China and South America which forces China to look for agriculture agriculture products in North America, United States and and Canada. Okay. Um also if there's a war that breaks out in Iran then c then China needs oil and the um and so China is now forced to buy oil from Canada and the United States. Okay. Okay, so that's one element of what's going on. Okay, that's number one. Number two is we have to remember what the Canadian economic model is. Okay, there are three pillars to the Canadian economic model. The first is sell resources to America. 77% of Canadian trade is with America. Okay, that's number one. Number two is exploit cheap immigrant labor. Right? Canadians don't actually work. They get immigrants to do to do the work for them. I know this because my dad was a dishwasher and had two managers who were Canadian. Okay, that's number two. But number three, which which people don't ignore is money laundering. Okay. Um, help corrupt officials from around the world launder their illgotten gains into Canadian real estate. Okay. So, the very important idea is these EVs are coming into Canada. They're meant to um be the spearhead for this massive collaboration uh between Chinese companies and Canadian companies which allows Chinese companies to monitor resources into Canada. So it's money laundering. Okay, that's one aspect. The other aspect is this. Look, I keep on saying this, but Canada has become a toxic asset and and where real estate is completely overpriced. And so they're trying to dump this toxic asset onto the Chinese. Okay. So what Carney really wants, okay, this is key, is access to the household savings of the average Chinese citizen, which is about 40%. So um in Canada, Canadians, the average Canadian household has has about 100% debt to income ratio. Okay? So um basically Canadians owe more than they actually earn. But in um in China um Chinese will save 40% of all the income. That's a huge financial resource for Carney to tap into. Okay. So what Carney wants, he doesn't care about the EVs. He doesn't care about selling canola to the Chinese. Who cares? That that that's opinions. What he really wants is a new economic relationship between Canada and China where the Chinese households are able to divert a lot of savings to Canadian resources. Okay, so imagine Chinese buying houses in Canada. Imagine Chinese sending their their kids to Canada for university. But also imagine Chinese for stable coins buying Canadian currency. Okay, so that I think is the grand plan because again Connie is a banker. He thinks like a banker. He he thinks about how to package a financial resource and sell it really expensive to stupid investors. Okay, that's how he thinks. >> Um so so I think that's a grand strategy here and Trump's going to go along with it because this is this for Trump is really the ideal arrangement between China and United States as well. So let Carney test the waters, see see how the Chinese respond and then when Trump visits Beijing in April, negotiate the same deal. And do you think the Chinese government appreciates fully that this is uh this is the long-term strategy of the banker? >> Okay. So um the decisions are not usually made I'm I'm talking like like highlevel decisions are not usually made between government and government. They're usually made between central bank and central bank. Okay. So um the bank of international settlements in Basil Switzerland that's a coordinating body of the central banks. Okay. Okay. And that's where they come together and they discuss their problems and the solutions. So you know the Canadians have a problem because you know it's I mean it's entirely based on money lottery. It's a Ponzi scheme. So they need you know people to come in and and and store their resources in Canada. Okay. That's that's how the thing works. The Chinese have a problem because their people refuse to spend their money. Okay. And Chinese refuse to spend their money because Chinese obviously no confidence in the future. there's no there's no safety net. Um the pension is not reliable. Um their children is not going to have a great bright future. So they need to save us up as much money as possible for uh the future. But the problem from from economic perspective is that that's actually uh counterproductive. You need people to spend their money for the economy to function, right? So the the money is is standing is staying in the bank dormant. That creates a lot of issues. One major issue is of course is trade imbalance where um where you know China has like 1.2 trillion trade um surplus with the rest of the world which is just it does it doesn't help the Chinese at all. Okay. So it so the Chinese central bank has a problem of like how to how to increase consumption. The Canadian central bank of has has a problem of how to um um absorb more of savings from the world. Okay. So so I think that these guys had serious discussions at the bank of international settlements. They've come to an agreement and that's what um and that's why Carney went to Beijing. Okay? Because remember there had to be an agreement in place for these leaders to meet. The two leaders meet in order to sign theou. They're not not going to sit together for like days and days to negotiate the deal. That's what underlings do. Okay. So I think there's already been an agreement in place and how this agreement will pan out is something that we will observe over the next year. >> Now u I want to focus on one particular aspect of this agreement. uh and namely the electrical vehicles. Uh I happen to drive an electrical car, but it isn't a Chinese-made one because it pretty much is inaccessible in this country. So now the BYD vehicles will be coming and based on what I've heard about them and read about them, they're going to be extraordinarily attractive probably to a lot of consumers in this country. And uh assuming that the quota doesn't get in the way, uh one could easily imagine a very rapid increase in the sales of Chinese EVs in Canada in the months and years ahead. Do you think that uh there's any prospect of uh Chinese automobile manufacturers uh moving production or some part of their production to Canada in order to avoid the destruction of the domestic automobile industry or uh is that not the way they operate? >> I I that's a plan. I I think that's agreement. I think the agreement is for Chinese EV factories to move over to Canada and create joint ventures. Okay. So the Canadian the sorry the Chinese political system it's very strange for westerners. So um EVs it's not an economic tool it's a political tool and what I mean by that is think of the 2008 Beijing Olympics where the government spent billions and billions of dollars on creating this amazing show for the world. And so it's a political show okay and that's what EVs are. EVs are a sign that China is technologically advanced that um it's not just a like a sweat shop to the world that it is one of the leading uh technological centers of the world and it's all part of China's like grand strategy to be a technological powerhouse. Okay. So um so the problem with EVs is that they're heavily subsidized by the government. So people don't appreciate this but EVs lose money. Okay? Because they're all heavily subsidized by the government and there's over capacity in China. So like everywhere you go, there are lots of EVs. Um but they can't actually sell all the EVs in um in China. So for Canada to start selling EVs, for Canadians to start buying Chinese EVs, for Chinese for Chinese EV companies to open shop in Canada, it would be a huge political victory for the Chinese leadership. and that's all they care about. >> Let's talk about another uh sector, telecommunications. You know, when Mark Carney concluded this trip, uh the neocons in Canada started freaking out. Uh and one expression of that was this article in the Hill Times, which uh is a go-to newspaper for Canada's political elite. It's based in Ottawa. And as you can see here, uh, the Hill Times went to security experts, uh, or at least that's the way they describe themselves, uh, including a former director of CEUS Canada spy agency and also to, uh, politicians and, uh, asked them about Huawei and there's a ban on Huawei's 5G in this country and, uh, they warned ominously that we should not lift that ban and uh, continued to express hostility to this really this extraordinary corporation. What do you think is going on here, Professor Jiang? Why is the Canadian political and economic elite uh and particularly the security establishment so hostile to Huawei? I mean, they claim that it's potentially a vehicle for the uh the PRC to spy on Canadians. Uh do you think that that's what's really underlying this hostility? >> Um so I think this is very personal. So remember um that uh there's bad blood between Huawei and Canada because uh a hallway executive um she she was I think a Canadian uh PR and she was living in Vancouver. Okay. But but she she is um she works for hallway and she's actually the her parent to the hallway empire u m Joe and when she was um going to Mexico for a meeting she had to transfer through Vancouver. She was detained by the Canadian authorities uh because the Americans requested uh that she be extradited and >> and that that was Donald Trump just to be clear. Sorry to interrupt you but it was the first administration. >> Yes. Yes. And the Chinese responded by basically holding two Canadians hostage.
